metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add proving commands for each of the four additional AC6 content requirements — e.g. `grep -q "Signed-off-by" CONTRIBUTING.md`, `grep -q "best-effort" CONTRIBUTING.md`, `grep -q "Co-authored-by\|Forge Author" CONTRIBUTING.md` (the last anchored on E2 outcome) — so all stated requirements have a verifiable check.
findings.finding: AC6's proving command (`grep -q "format-patch" CONTRIBUTING.md`) only checks for a single substring but the criterion requires four additional content elements: 'both-trailer DCO handling', 'authorship preservation per E2 outcome', 'trailer policy', and 'best-effort caveat'. No proving command covers those remaining requirements, meaning the AC can be marked green while the section is substantively incomplete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC6 to: `grep -q 'format-patch' CONTRIBUTING.md && grep -q 'Signed-off-by' CONTRIBUTING.md && grep -q 'best-effort' CONTRIBUTING.md && grep -qE 'Co-authored-by|Forge Author' CONTRIBUTING.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify step to AC2 confirming the workflow message template includes the PR author's handle token (e.g., `grep -q 'github.event.pull_request.user.login' .github/workflows/lockdown.yml` or equivalent) to cover the 'thank by name' requirement.
findings.finding: The plan's five-part message skeleton explicitly includes 'thank by name' (using the PR opener's GitHub handle) as part 1, but AC2's verify steps check only the middle three content elements ('read-only mirror', 'nothing is wrong with your change', Gerrit link, docs link, patch-fallback offer) — the 'thank by name' requirement is described work with no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section — comment message paragraph; Acceptance Criteria AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'message template references the PR opener by name — prove: `grep -q "github.event.pull_request.user.login" .github/workflows/lockdown.yml` (or the dessant/repo-lockdown equivalent interpolation token if the action handles it natively).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for input-key verification in AC3, e.g. `curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dessant/repo-lockdown/<sha>/action.yml | yq '.inputs | keys' | diff - <(grep -oP '(?<=with:)\n\s+\K\w+' .github/workflows/lockdown.yml)` or equivalent `yq`/`grep` diff against the pinned action.yml's `inputs:` block.
findings.finding: AC3 states 'input keys verified against the pinned action.yml' but provides no concrete proving command for this check. The plan narrative notes that a renamed input 'fails silently', making this verification safety-critical, yet the AC gives the executor no deterministic command to produce evidence of verification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `has_pull_requests` is a real settable GitHub REST API field (check the PATCH `/repos/{owner}/{repo}` schema and cross-reference the existing infra scripts that patch the API), and if not, identify the correct mechanism for the Feb-2026 'disable PRs' feature before writing E1 and the ADR 0037 rejected-alternative rationale.
findings.finding: Experiment E1 references testing 'the native Feb-2026 `has_pull_requests: false` setting' as a real GitHub API field, but the repo's own GitHub API scripts (`infra/github/rollback-mirror-lock.sh`, `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`) use `has_issues`, `has_projects`, `has_wiki` — never `has_pull_requests`. The GitHub Repositories API does not expose a `has_pull_requests` toggle; the correct mechanism for the Feb-2026 feature is a repository-level setting surfaced only through the web UI or a different API path. The plan cites this as justification for the peer-comparison decision (native disable = 'zero-cost but silent'), so an incorrect capability claim here undermines the documented ADR rationale.
findings.location: Why section: 'Chromium/Qt/torvalds flipped GitHub's native Feb-2026 `has_pull_requests: false` setting'; E1 pre-flight: 'Scratch-repo test of `has_pull_requests: false` fork-side UX'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `has_pull_requests: false` reference with the correct API/UI mechanism (e.g. the GraphQL `updateRepository` mutation or the web Settings toggle introduced with the Feb-2026 GitHub feature), and redesign E1 to test that actual mechanism on a scratch repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to E1's framing, verify the exact GitHub API field name (or UI-only path) for disabling PRs on a repository — confirm whether `has_pull_requests` is a writable REST API key or a UI-only setting, and update E1's test procedure and ADR 0037's rejection evidence accordingly.
findings.finding: E1 instructs the executor to test 'fork-side UX' of GitHub's native `has_pull_requests: false` setting, which the plan frames as a real 'Feb-2026 native' API toggle. However, `has_pull_requests` is not a writable field in the GitHub Repositories REST API (`PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}`); the API exposes `has_issues`, `has_projects`, `has_wiki`, and `has_discussions` but not PR enablement via this key. If E1 cannot be executed (no such API field), the ADR 0037 'rejected alternative' section will record a capability that does not exist, undermining the decision record's integrity.
findings.location: Pre-flight experiments — E1; ADR 0037 scope ('native disable rejection (E1 evidence)')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the E1 API assumption with: check the GitHub REST API docs for the `PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}` schema (the `allow_forking`, `has_issues`, etc. fields) to find the actual PR-disable mechanism. If it is UI-only (Settings → General → Pull Requests checkbox), document that the E1 test must be done via the web UI, not via `gh api`, and record a screenshot rather than a CLI command as ADR evidence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what document or artifact E4's 'outsider-ticket flow' cross-check should use if `docs/your-first-change.md` is not yet available at the time E4 runs — either point to the relevant CONTRIBUTING.md section, or make E4 explicitly conditional on the onboarding story landing first.
findings.finding: E4's cross-check target — 'the onboarding tutorial's outsider-ticket flow' — is `docs/your-first-change.md`, which does not exist; E4 cannot be executed as specified because the artifact it references is absent.
findings.location: Pre-flight experiment E4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace (or augment) AC2's source-grep proof with a live check of the test PR's actual posted comment body (e.g., `gh pr view <PR#> --comments | grep 'read-only mirror'`), so message-content verification exercises the real action output rather than the YAML source.
findings.finding: AC2's message-content verification is a source-grep of the workflow file (`lockdown.yml`), not an inspection of the comment actually posted on the test PR. This is a self-authored-oracle / proxy-validation anti-pattern: the grep confirms the YAML was written with the right strings, but cannot catch the failure modes the plan itself acknowledges ('a renamed input fails silently', action truncation, template expansion errors). The live path — what the bot actually posts — is never verified end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('prove by grep of the workflow file')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, change 'prove by grep of the workflow file' to 'prove by inspecting the test PR created in AC1: `gh pr view <PR#> --comments` output contains "read-only mirror", "nothing is wrong with your change", a link to docs/your-first-change.md, a link to https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com, and the patch-fallback offer; and contains none of "rejected", "not accepted", "closed as invalid".' The source-grep can remain as a secondary static check but should not be the sole oracle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in AC4 that the preferred proof is a real Dependabot-authored PR NOT being closed (e.g., trigger a Dependabot version-bump by temporarily lowering a dependency version bound), and document that simulation is only an acceptable fallback with explicit rationale if Dependabot cannot produce a PR in the verification environment.
findings.finding: AC4 explicitly accepts 'a simulated actor test' as sufficient proof that Dependabot PRs are not closed. A local simulation (e.g. `act` or a crafted workflow_dispatch with a faked actor) does not exercise the live GitHub Actions environment where `github.actor` is populated by GitHub's infrastructure, meaning the exemption `if: github.actor != 'dependabot[bot]'` could behave differently in production without any AC catching the divergence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `docs/your-first-change.md` dependency: either (a) add the prerequisite story/ticket ID as an explicit `depends_on` link so ordering is enforced, or (b) specify a fallback URL (e.g., CONTRIBUTING.md anchor) to use in the bot comment until that file exists.
findings.finding: `docs/your-first-change.md` does not exist in the repository, yet the plan's bot-comment message deep-links it as a live URL contributors will immediately follow after being redirected, and AC2's content-grep proof checks for a link to this file in `lockdown.yml`. The plan acknowledges this dependency ('exists before this lands — this story depends_on the onboarding story') but does not name the prerequisite story/ticket, making the dependency unverifiable and the AC unprovable if the onboarding story hasn't landed.
findings.location: What section (bot comment message: 'deep-link Gerrit ... + docs/your-first-change.md'); AC2 (message content check)
findings.suggested_fix: Add the onboarding story ticket ID as an explicit `depends_on` on this ticket, and change the bot comment to link CONTRIBUTING.md with a `#contributing-via-patch` anchor as a fallback until `docs/your-first-change.md` exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the E2 block whether an absent Forge Author grant is handled IN this story (add it here, with an explicit note that it counts as in-scope infra work) or ticketed separately via `discovered_from` so the bot/policy work can ship independently; the current text leaves both options open and could cause mid-execution scope creep.
findings.finding: E2 creates a conditional scope expansion: if Forge Author is NOT granted to Registered Users, the plan says 'add the grant via the declarative config path' (editing infra/gerrit/project.config), but it is ambiguous whether that infra change belongs inside this story or in a separate ticket. If the grant is absent and the maintainer chooses to add it here, this story now carries a second independently-releasable outcome (a Gerrit ACL infrastructure change) with a distinct reason-to-change (infra configuration vs. contributor-intake UX).
findings.location: Pre-flight experiments, E2 paragraph; also CONTRIBUTING.md shepherded-patch section ('If E2 finds Forge Author NOT grantable...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the E2 bullet: 'If the grant must be added and is non-trivial (requires a separate review cycle), create a child ticket via `discovered_from` and downgrade the authorship promise in this story for now — do not block the intake workflow on ACL infra.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability/verification step: either a post-run assertion in the workflow (e.g. confirm the PR is in 'closed' state via `gh pr view`) that fails loudly on mismatch, or document a monitoring approach (e.g. GitHub Actions failure notifications) so silent action failures are surfaced to the maintainer.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly acknowledges that a renamed input in the pinned action.yml 'fails silently', but provides no mitigation: no CI smoke test against the pinned SHA's manifest, no workflow-run failure alerting, and no fallback behavior if the action exits without closing/commenting. A silent failure leaves PRs open and unanswered — the exact failure mode the plan is designed to prevent — with no operator visibility.
findings.location: What section — lockdown.yml spec: 'Verify the v5 input key names against the action's manifest (action.yml) at execution; a renamed input fails silently'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second workflow step after the repo-lockdown action that calls `gh pr view ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --json state -q .state` and asserts the value is `CLOSED`, failing the workflow run (and triggering GitHub's native failed-run notification) if it is not. This converts a silent no-op into a loud, observable failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `docs/your-first-change.md` is delivered (or explicitly placeholder-committed) before `lockdown.yml` goes live, and add a CI/AC step that confirms the linked file resolves in the repo (not just that the string appears in the workflow YAML).
findings.finding: `docs/your-first-change.md` does not exist in the repository, but the plan asserts it '(exists before this lands)' as a precondition for the bot comment deep-link and AC2 content check.
findings.location: What section — bot comment message spec; also AC2 ('a link to `docs/your-first-change.md`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit sequencing gate: make this story depend_on the onboarding story's completion (or add a placeholder `docs/your-first-change.md` as a deliverable of this story if the onboarding story may slip), and add `test -f docs/your-first-change.md` as a verifiable AC sub-check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a delivery-order AC to `lockdown.yml`'s acceptance criteria (e.g., 'AC0: `docs/your-first-change.md` exists and resolves at merge time — verified by `curl -f` or a file-existence check in the PR gate') so the link in the bot comment is guaranteed live before this story merges.
findings.finding: The bot comment mechanism deep-links `docs/your-first-change.md` — a file confirmed absent from the repository — and the plan's only guard is the informal note '(exists before this lands — this story depends_on the onboarding story)'. AC2 verifies the link string appears inside `lockdown.yml` (a grep of the workflow file), not that the linked target resolves. If this story lands before the onboarding story, every contributor redirected by the bot immediately hits a 404 at the critical trust-building moment after their PR is closed. The plan's stated dependency is not enforced by any acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section — bot comment spec; AC2 (Message content check)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC: 'AC0 (precondition): `docs/your-first-change.md` is present in the repo at merge time — prove: `test -f docs/your-first-change.md`. If the onboarding story has not landed, substitute a stub doc that redirects to CONTRIBUTING.md so the link never 404s.' This makes the informal `depends_on` a hard gate rather than an advisory note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `dessant/repo-lockdown` v5 input key names (`close-pr`, `lock-pr`, comment input) by reading the v5 `action.yml` directly NOW (e.g. `curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dessant/repo-lockdown/<sha>/action.yml`) and record confirmed key names in the plan before writing the workflow.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `close-pr: true`, `lock-pr: false`, and an implicit comment-message input for `dessant/repo-lockdown@<sha>` (v5) but these input key names are asserted without being verified against the v5 `action.yml` manifest. If v5 renamed any input (e.g. `close` vs `close-pr`, or a different comment key), the workflow executes without error but silently does nothing — every PR intake AC fails with no diagnostic.
findings.location: What section — lockdown workflow spec: '`close-pr: true`, `lock-pr: false` (LibreOffice pattern)'; AC3 'input keys verified against the pinned action.yml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Resolved capability' sub-bullet under the lockdown workflow spec that cites the observed input key names from the pinned action.yml, converting the deferred-to-execution note into a pre-resolved design decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground E2 against the observed `infra/gerrit/project.config` baseline (no `forgeAuthor` entry exists today) and explicitly state what config change E2 would make and at which ACL scope (project vs. All-Projects vs. server global), so the go/no-go decision is actionable rather than open-ended.
findings.finding: The plan's E2 pre-flight experiment asserts that 'Forge Author' may be grantable to Registered Users on the Gerrit rebar project, and makes the shepherded-patch authorship promise conditional on that outcome — but the live `infra/gerrit/project.config` has NO `forgeAuthor` ACL entry anywhere, and the plan does not acknowledge this absence as the baseline from which E2 departs. This is an unverified capability assertion about the Gerrit server configuration: the capability may require All-Projects config changes or server-level permissions that are outside the scope of the declarative project.config path the plan names.
findings.location: What section — E2 pre-flight: 'is Forge Author granted to Registered Users on the rebar project? If not, add the grant via the declarative config path'; CONTRIBUTING.md shepherded-patch authorship promise

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete pre-flight verification step (E-series or AC3 sub-step) that fetches and greps the pinned `dessant/repo-lockdown` action.yml at the resolved SHA to confirm `close-pr` and `lock-pr` are the exact input key names before merging `lockdown.yml`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `dessant/repo-lockdown` inputs `close-pr: true` and `lock-pr: false` as the v5 key names but explicitly defers verification to 'execution time' with no CI-integrated check; a renamed input fails silently (the plan's own words) and there is no AC step or pre-flight experiment that reads the pinned action's action.yml to confirm the exact key names before the workflow goes live.
findings.location: What section — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` description; AC3 ('Workflow hardening — prove: … input keys verified against the pinned action.yml')
findings.suggested_fix: In the E3 or a new E5 step, add: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dessant/repo-lockdown/<resolved-sha>/action.yml | grep -E '^  (close-pr|lock-pr):'` and record the output as a ticket comment. Alternatively, add a workflow-level smoke test job that runs `yq eval '.inputs | keys' <lockdown-action.yml>` against the pinned SHA and asserts the required keys exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit spec constraint to `lockdown.yml`'s definition stating the workflow MUST NOT include `actions/checkout` or any step that materialises fork-branch code/content into a shell execution context, and note this as a security invariant in a comment within the workflow file.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `pull_request_target` (which executes with the base repo's elevated GITHUB_TOKEN even on fork PRs) and acknowledges the elevated-token risk, but never explicitly prohibits or documents the absence of `actions/checkout` of fork-branch code within the workflow. Checking out fork code under `pull_request_target` with a write-scoped token is the canonical RCE/exfiltration vector (GHSL-2021-* series; GitHub's own security advisory). The plan's least-privilege `permissions` block reduces blast radius but does not eliminate the risk if a future editor adds a checkout step. The spec should close this loop by explicitly stating the workflow contains no `actions/checkout` of fork code (or any step that materialises fork-controlled content into a shell context).
findings.location: What section — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` specification; `pull_request_target: types: [opened, reopened]` trigger definition
findings.suggested_fix: In the `lockdown.yml` spec block, add: 'This workflow intentionally contains no `actions/checkout` step and no shell interpolation of fork-controlled content (PR title/body). This is a security invariant for `pull_request_target`: the workflow only calls the `dessant/repo-lockdown` action with static inputs.' Add a corresponding `# SECURITY: no checkout of fork code` comment to the workflow YAML.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the named four-file enumeration from E3 (or expand it to all 11 current workflows) and rely solely on the `grep -l "pull_request" .github/workflows/*` command as the source of truth, so no file is silently excluded from the audit.
findings.finding: E3's 'known set' enumeration of `pull_request`-triggered workflows is incomplete — the plan names only four files (test.yml, optionality.yml, eval.yml, eval-plan-review-container.yml) but `.github/workflows/` contains at least seven additional files that are unaudited for PR-event triggers.
findings.location: Pre-flight experiment E3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'known set today: test.yml, optionality.yml, eval.yml, eval-plan-review-container.yml' with 'run `grep -l "pull_request" .github/workflows/*` at execution to get the authoritative set; do not pre-enumerate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `0037` is not already claimed by another in-flight plan (the cross-ticket overlap epic 0f70 targets `docs/adr/0037-cross-ticket-overlap.md`); if so, advance this ADR to `0038-oss-contribution-intake-posture.md` and update AC7 accordingly.
findings.finding: `docs/adr/0037-oss-contribution-intake-posture.md` is the plan's target ADR path, but the `docs/adr/` directory already has two files numbered `0036-*` (a dual-numbering collision exists at 0036), and a separate in-flight plan (epic 0f70) already claims `docs/adr/0037-cross-ticket-overlap.md` for the cross-ticket overlap feature. Two plans targeting the same number prefix will produce a filename collision on landing.
findings.location: What section: 'record as ADR docs/adr/0037-oss-contribution-intake-posture.md'; AC7: 'ADR 0037 committed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/pull_request_template.md` to the plan's scope (explicitly IN or OUT) and, if IN, enumerate any wording updates needed to stay consistent with the new bot-comment message.
findings.finding: `.github/pull_request_template.md` is an existing contributor-funnel file (confirmed present) that already delivers redirect messaging ('GitHub PRs are not merged here', 'read-only mirror') — the same message the new bot comment delivers. It is not listed in the plan's IN scope or OUT scope, nor flagged as a consumer to keep consistent with the new bot-comment wording.
findings.location: Scope section (IN/OUT); What section (comment message specification)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
